questions.prompt: What's the core risk the lesson names with exclusions?
choices: Exclusions are persistent, they live until someone removes them.

choices.label: Exclusions cost the MSP money per filing.
choices.rationale: Fabricated commercial mechanic.

choices.label: Exclusions expire after 30 days automatically.
choices.rationale: The opposite of the lesson, exclusions persist until removed.

choices.label: Exclusions are visible to the customer's IT, which the customer dislikes.
choices.rationale: Not the lesson's risk framing.

questions.explanation: If a pattern that was benign last month becomes part of attacker tradecraft this month, the exclusion that suppressed the old benign version also suppresses the new malicious version. Persistence is the source of the risk. That's why the bar for filing is conditions plus narrow scope.

questions.prompt: Which combination of conditions makes an exclusion appropriate per the lesson?
choices: Same legitimate activity has fired multiple SOC-classified-benign incidents AND the customer's environment has stabilised AND the activity is genuinely characteristic of the customer AND the exclusion would be narrow.

choices.label: Customer has asked for it AND the activity has fired at least once.
choices.rationale: Customer pressure alone isn't a legitimate trigger. Single firing isn't the bar.

choices.label: The activity is PowerShell-related AND the source is the RMM.
choices.rationale: Pattern-based filing, not classification-based.

choices.label: The activity has fired in the last 24 hours AND the senior approves.
choices.rationale: Recent firing isn't the bar; consistent prior classification is.

questions.explanation: All four conditions together, not any one alone.

questions.prompt: Which of these is the MOST COMMON new-tech failure on exclusions per the lesson?
choices: Filing exclusion requests during the first-week noise window.

choices.label: Filing exclusion requests after the customer is offboarded.
choices.rationale: Wrong category. Offboarding isn't the noise-window case.

choices.label: Filing exclusions for SIEM data sources.
choices.rationale: Not named as the most common failure.

choices.label: Filing exclusions in writing rather than verbally.
choices.rationale: The lesson endorses the written documented path.

questions.explanation: Because 'five incidents on the same thing seems efficient to exclude'. The SOC is still classifying during the noise window. Wait.

questions.prompt: Why is filing an exclusion mid-Critical (or immediately after) explicitly wrong?
choices: The activity that just fired Critical is exactly the activity you don't want to suppress.

choices.label: Huntress charges extra for exclusions filed during an active incident.
choices.rationale: Fabricated commercial mechanic.

choices.label: The exclusion would only apply to future incidents.
choices.rationale: That's actually how all exclusions work; not the reason for the timing rule.

choices.label: The senior isn't available to review exclusions during a Critical.
choices.rationale: Not the structural reason.

questions.explanation: Even if it turns out to be a false positive. The false-positive determination should be the SOC's classification, not the tech's exclusion. Suppressing the exact pattern that just fired Critical is the worst class of exclusion.

questions.prompt: Which exclusion shape does the lesson endorse versus reject?
choices: Endorses 'exclude this specific scheduled task by name';

choices.label: Endorses 'exclude PowerShell on WS-EXAMPLE-IT-02'; rejects per-task names.
choices.rationale: Inverted. Broad exclusions are the named common mistake.

choices.label: Endorses both shapes equally.
choices.rationale: Wrong. The narrow-vs-broad distinction is load-bearing.

choices.label: Endorses 'exclude all SIEM logs from this customer'.
choices.rationale: Catastrophically broad. Disables a whole detection surface.

questions.explanation: Rejects 'exclude PowerShell on WS-EXAMPLE-IT-02'. Narrow exclusions earn approval; broad exclusions create blind spots.

questions.prompt: Order the exclusion review flow from the lesson.
items: Tech files the request via the documented exclusion-request path (PSA ticket category, portal form, or designated Slack channel).
Senior reviews against the four-condition bar.
Senior submits to Huntress, or tech submits with senior approval.
SOC reviews and either applies the exclusion, applies a narrower version, or declines with reasoning.
questions.explanation: Documented path, senior review, SOC approval. Each gate is there for a reason; suppression-by-closing bypasses all of them.

questions.prompt: Riverbend Legal has had eight Low EDR incidents over two weeks for the same scheduled task, each SOC-classified benign. Noise window ended four months ago, environment is stable. What's the right action?

choices.label: Keep triaging each instance as a routine Low, the workflow is the workflow.
choices.rationale: Defensible but missing the opportunity. Eight repeat benign incidents on a stable customer is the legitimate exclusion case.

choices: File an exclusion request via the MSP's documented path.

choices.label: Close the next instance as 'benign, dismissing' without filing a request.
choices.rationale: Suppression-by-closing bypasses the documented flow; the SOC doesn't get told to stop firing.

choices.label: File a broad exclusion: 'ignore all scheduled tasks on this customer'.
choices.rationale: Wrong shape. Broad exclusions create the gap you'd hate to have when the actual attacker shows up.

questions.explanation: Include the specific scheduled-task name, the eight prior incident IDs, customer IT confirmation, and the narrowness of the proposed scope. Conditions met, request narrow, evidence documented. Senior reviews; SOC applies if approved.

questions.prompt: Customer's IT manager calls: "We've changed our nightly inventory script to a new one called Riverbend-Daily-Inventory-Sync-v2. Please add that to the exclusion too." What's the right answer?

choices.label: Yes, file the exclusion for the new name immediately, since we already have one for v1.
choices.rationale: Wrong. The new script hasn't been SOC-classified yet; no history of 'fired and classified benign multiple times'.

choices: Explain that the new script will likely fire as a routine Low for the first few incidents while the SOC classifies;

choices.label: Tell the customer 'we can't suppress anything without going through process' and end the conversation.
choices.rationale: Defensible but missing the customer-relationship piece. The full answer gives them the eventual yes plus the why-not-immediately.

choices.label: File an exclusion for both the old and new script names, plus any future versions.
choices.rationale: Catastrophically wrong shape. Future-versions exclusion is broad; the new name hasn't been classified.

questions.explanation: Once classified benign across multiple incidents, the existing exclusion can be updated or a new one filed. Honest about the process, gives the customer the expectation, doesn't compromise the classification bar.

questions.prompt: "Our IT manager wants you to stop alerting on X." Is this a legitimate exclusion trigger?
choices: Only if X has been classified as benign by the SOC across multiple incidents and the other conditions are met.

choices.label: Yes, customer preferences are authoritative on their own environment.
choices.rationale: Named common mistake. Customer pressure as sole driver is the failure mode.

choices.label: Yes, but only if the customer is paying for the premium support tier.
choices.rationale: Fabricated commercial mechanic.

choices.label: No, customer preferences are irrelevant to exclusion decisions.
choices.rationale: Too strong. Customer context can be supporting evidence; it's not the trigger but it's also not irrelevant.

questions.explanation: Customer preference alone isn't the trigger. The trigger is the SOC's classification history plus the four conditions; customer preference is at most contextual.

questions.prompt: When should the tech ESCALATE to a senior instead of filing the exclusion request themselves?
choices: Customer pushing hard for an exclusion that doesn't meet the bar;

choices.label: Whenever the exclusion would affect more than one host.
choices.rationale: Multi-host scope isn't the criterion; broad-pattern scope is. A narrow exclusion across many hosts can still meet the bar.

choices.label: Whenever the tech has been on the platform for less than 6 months.
choices.rationale: Experience-based gating isn't the lesson's framing.

choices.label: Never, exclusion requests are always senior-led from the start.
choices.rationale: Wrong. The tech files the request; the senior reviews.

questions.explanation: Proposed exclusion would be broad; exclusion would suppress a pattern the senior is investigating across the portfolio; tech is not sure whether the conditions are met. Four named cases for escalating instead of filing.

questions.prompt: Closing repeat incidents as "benign, dismissing" without filing a documented exclusion request is the same thing as filing an exclusion.
questions.explanation: Suppression-by-closing bypasses the senior review and the documented flow. The SOC doesn't get told to stop firing; the next incident still hits the queue and just gets a more dismissive close.
